messages.author: Unknown
messages.content:
For comics specifically i personally think it's best to write it as a script. What i do is i kinda write it first like a summary I'm telling someone else so i don't worry about sounding poetic.

Then i write it like a movie script. 

I think taking screenwriting courses will definitely help. But really any fiction writing course will help.

The best part about being both the artist and author for a comic is you don't need to worry if someone else can understand your script. As long as you know for sure you'll understand what you mean later.

messages.content:
**🔸The Shading Course

✅ How to Control Tonal Value
Learn how to organize tonal values, how to see values more accurately, and how to deal with the limited tonal range of your medium.

✅How to Create Realistic Shading
Learn how light interacts with form to create luminous, clean shading. Develop the technical skills necessary to produce professional artwork.

✅How to Show Form
Learn how to think like a sculptor and use 7 methods for maximizing the sense of three-dimensional volume in your drawings.

✅Understand Scene Lighting
Learn how to use light probes to analyze the lighting in a scene and create believable shading, even when shading from imagination.

✅How to Invent Shading
Learn how to create realistic shading from imagination.

✅How to Use Maquettes
Learn how to create digital and physical shading references to bring your imaginative artwork to the next level.

**#Tutorial

🖥 [Home Page](https://www.proko.com/course/the-shading-course)

messages.author: Unknown
messages.content: This costs money but it's been a great help for me in learning anatomy - https://3d4medical.com/. Basically a 3D model of the entire human body made for medical students. Shows you all the bones, muscles, and their insertion points and you can isolate things to see where they connect/end. I've been drawing muscles on top of all my figures with this side by side and it's great for memorizing them.

messages.author: Unknown
messages.content: All I can say is to be patient and take it slowly. At least it's working for me. No need to rush, especially the first lessons where he talks about the stickman and the proportions in perspective. I think those are the most important concepts to learn early on to understand the rest of the course. First half of the course is aimed to make you understand volumes, and how to simplify anatomy and get better spatial awareness. It's very important you don't rush videos, and practice along him helped very much. Watch, pause, practice, understand, rinse and repeat. I started in January and no gonna lie I feel frustrated at the beginning. Then everything came all together, because after the anatomy section he teaches you also how to use shapes to establish the base of the figure, putting all together, and all the knowledge about volumes and perspective you already learned will make it very intuitive. At least, this is my experience. And sorry for my English.
